preamble: The palatalized voiceless alveolar fricative /sʲ/, characterized by the raising of the tongue body towards the hard palate, can undergo a historical phonetic transformation to a voiceless postalveolar fricative /ʃ/. This shift often involves a retraction of the tongue tip or blade further back in the mouth, resulting in a more 'hushing' sound.

examples.note: In the historical development of Russian, the Proto-Slavic second-person singular verb ending, which included a palatalized alveolar fricative, evolved into the modern form with a voiceless retroflex fricative /ʂ/ (a type of postalveolar fricative).

sources: How can the continuum s-sʲ-ç-ɕ-ʃ-ʂ be described in technical terms? - Linguistics Stack Exchange. (2021, January 3).
